**

Carousel Ranking Model

Xiaochang Miao, Josh Zhu

# Problem

The content in our homepage has 

We want to understand:

- How many carousels are optimal for consumers?
    
- How to rank those carousels?
    
- How to deal with operational input rules in place?
    

# Success Metrics and Opportunity

- Increase global conversion rate by 0.25% or more
    
- Increase D7-14OF
    

  

Carousel ranking will enable DoorDash to invest in a comprehensive [content strategy](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1dd_Q1sM16-fdoP__hxZWGA8Tg6prRUAtQMHhu9Y_tpo/edit) → the right carousel for each user segment, mood, daypart, and food profile. The ranking / personalization strategy, paired with abundant carousel candidates, will significantly improve our ability to help users discover and connect with the right merchants, and come back more frequently.

# Business Sizing

For the first version of the ranker, we expect a 0.1% global conversion lift (assuming similar sizing as cuisine filter personalization) [analysis](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1iIT1V3ZDW-R882ZC0dWj6H5TdoyRmg-Doz4FrK7kBvo/edit?ts=5f0614dc#gid=497294630).

  

The expected impact in global conversion for V1 does not account for new carousels (content) in the app.

  

# MVP Model Specification

## Estimated Probability of Converting from a Carousel

### Algorithm

Step 1 

We can calibrate the score we predicted from the LR model for each store to the probability (likelihood) that the Cx will convert from that store. Using a method similar to what’s described [here](https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.calibration.calibration_curve.html).

  

Step 2

For a given carousel, since to which the belongings of the stores are defined. We can estimate the conversion probability from that carousel to be (assume Store 1...N belong to Carousel A and will be displayed in homepage):

P(converting from Carousel A) = 1-P(No conversion from Carousel A) =1-iN(1-wi*P(storei))

w1...wN is the discount factors that we will discount the probability of conversion given the position i of the store in the carousel, vs. if it’s at the 1st position of the carousel, i.e. w1 = 1

  

The discount factors can be estimated offline:

1. Estimate the expected impression discount ratios using this [query](https://app.snowflake.com/us-west-2/doordash/w4AiB2uUH1Mi#query) according to the position in the carousel
    
2. Estimate the impression-to-conversion ratio using this [query](https://app.snowflake.com/us-west-2/doordash/w4nXuC76UjAM#query), noting that the impression for position 1 - 9 needs to be adjusted by using the discount ratio from step 1 * impression for position 0, this is because the impression is only logged when Cx scrolled and viewed the card, not capturing the stores they did not scroll and view
    
3. Discount ratios for position 1 - 9 will the adjusted impression-to-conversion ratios of position 1 - 9 divided by the impression-to-conversion ratio of position 0
    

  

For deal carousels, we are treating them the same as normal carousel, except have a different estimate of w1...wN

### Features

- Same as all [existing consumer / store features](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1lLlMC2aZyOk6RCbzk--MDDg6CiC3u4RshEwQvDEV6EY/edit#slide=id.g30c9fa3ce2_0_300) in the current LR ranking model
    

### Pros & Cons

Pros:

- Easy model development and implementation (can get rid of Sibyl dependencies)
    
- Store-specific, agnostic to the day-part and other dynamic factors which change the belongings of stores in a carousel
    
- No storage required
    

Cons:

- Rely on existing LR models, the behavior might be different for carousel vs. store feed, in terms of vertical layout vs. horizontal layout
    
- Can only work for conversion based models
    
- We are making the assumption that if two carousels have the same list of stores / same order will behave similarly. In reality the title of the carousel will have influence. 
    

  

### Product Requirements

- How will this model handle the cold-start problem? (i.e. we launch a brand-new carousel, how do we rank it?) 
    

→ The algorithm’s granularity is at store level, therefore it would treat any new carousel as the rest. In a way, it’s treating a carousel as a set of stores.

  

- How will this model consider sort order overrides and carousel position boosting? 
    

→ This algorithm can be overridden by any manual sort overrides

  

- How will this model rank algorithmic, declarative and manual carousels?
    

→ No difference in treating different types of carousels. 

  

- How will this model be able to handle the following carousels campaigns: 
    

- Cuisine carousels (best burger spots, etc)
    
- Food category: Healthy Picks
    
- Trendy / Popular carousels
    
- Dayparted carousels: Top breakfast picks, etc.
    
- User-targeted carousels: e.g. Now on DashPass
    
- Collection Carousels (coming in late Q3)
    

→ No difference in treating different types of carousels.

  

Post-processing after ranking:

- We currently have the deduping logic that removes the same store from the carousel which ranked below which are X carousels apart. 
    

- We need to look at how often those dedupe happen (no logging so far)
    
- We need to look at the impact of this on carousel ranking
    

- We need to think about diversity after more similar types of carousels are present 
    

### Modeling/Production Effort

- 2 wks modeling + offline estimate
    
- 1 wk of real-time production changes
    

### Eng Dependency:

- No hard dependency can work with existing infrastructure
    

  

# Experiment Plan

We also want to find the optimal number of carousels presented (after relevance ranking) to consumers through online experiments. 

  

We can run the experiment with carousel ranker algorithm we designed. The proposed setting is:

1. Control: default ranking + default number of presented carousels
    
2. Treatment-1: new ranking model + default number of presented carousels
    
3. Treatment-2: new ranking model + 4 most relevant carousels
    
4. Treatment-2: new ranking model + 5 most relevant carousels
    
5. Treatment-3: new ranking model + 6 most relevant carousels
    

  

The timeline of this experiment can start on 08/24 and run for two weeks

  
  
  
  

# V2 - Longer Term Modeling Exploration

### Algorithm

In V2, the goal is to train a supervised machine learning model to predict the probability of view-to-conversion for each carousel when populating with a set of pre-selected stores.  The model architecture is shown in the below figure.

  

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Fig. Holistic Model Architecture for Carousel Ranking

  

To begin with, we can utilize both store and consumer features from the existing store conversion ranking model (LR15ED). For each carousel, take the top-k stores and aggregate both store embeddings and numerical features by either assigning higher weights to the top-ranked stores or learn those weights during the model training process.

  

In addition, we can also add contextual and geoinformation, eg. daypart, carousel title/meta tags and geo-location, etc, as input. 

  

In the future, we can change the model architecture from single-task to multi-task, if we want to rank not only by conversion but also by other business metrics (eg. retention, order frequency).

  

### Features

Could we flesh out the features we are considering for this model?

  

### Addressing Product Requirements

- How will this model handle the cold-start problem? (i.e. we launch a brand-new carousel, how do we rank it?)
    
- How will this model consider sort order overrides and carousel position boosting?
    
- How will this model rank algorithmic, declarative and manual carousels?
    
- How will this model be able to handle the following carousels campaigns: 
    

- Food carousels (best burger spots, etc)
    
- Dayparted carousels: Top breakfast picks, etc.
    
- User-targeted carousels: e.g. Now on DashPass
    
- Collection Carousels (coming in late Q3)
    

### Modeling/Production Effort 

- 1 DSML, 2 - 3wks offline model development + performance tuning
    
- 1-Day for model deployment + experiment setup if Sibyl + Dynamic Values are ready
    

### Eng Dependency

- Need use Sibyl for real-time scoring
    
- Dynamic Values integration with carousel ranking experiment
    
- Need to have ranking move to Feed Service
    

  
  

# Appendix: Other MVP Models Considered

## Option 1: MAB with Thompson Sampling

### Algorithm

For every carousel and every user, draw the estimated view to conversion rate p from Beta(𝛼, 𝛽) distribution, with 𝛼 being the number of times a user has seen the carousel and also ended up purchasing from there, 𝛽 being the number of times a user has seen the carousel but didn’t purchase from there.

  

For every user, we rank by the p for each carousel and update 𝛼 and 𝛽 accordingly.

  

If we have business rules in place for certain carousels, we can manually adjust p for those carousels by a positive number (𝛾) from 0 to 1 depending on how much we want the ranking to be dominated by the business rules.

### Features (simplest) (view to conversion)

1. Number of views per carousel
    
2. Number of conversions per carousel
    

### Pros & Cons

Pros:

- Easy model development and implementation (can get rid of Sibyl dependencies)
    
- A balance between exploration and exploitation => Exploit the referred carousels (know with high confidence) to the consumers, while also give the opportunities to explore new carousels (don’t know for sure yet whether a consumer is going like it or not).
    
- Adaptive to the changes in consumer’s intent over time.
    
- Demonstrated success with cuisine filters (0.17% global conversion lift)
    

Cons:

- Massive Storage. Assuming if we have N consumers and M carousels, then it is O(N*M) space complexity.
    
- Don’t consider the store context within each carousel.
    
- We may end up ranking it too high / low depending on the time of day / geo.
    

1. Potential avenues to improve: use time / geo to compute the features (e.g. views and conversions)
    

  

### Modeling/Production Effort 

- 1DSML + 1wk offline model development
    
- 1 day - Eng effort for deployment + experiment kick-off
    

### Addressing Product Requirements

- Handling cold-start:
    

- For new consumers, we can fall back to the district-level preference.
    
- For new carousels, MAB + TS method will automatically take care of it by modeling with variations. For new carousels, since it has little data, the estimated probabilities of conversion are with high variance (ranking scores are likely to be higher than normal). Due to that, those new carousels will get more exposure to the consumers to get more statistics, and thus the variance will reduce overtime. 
    

- How will this model consider sort order overrides and carousel position boosting?
    

- We can reserve a placeholder for operational rules for merchandising purposes. The ultimate ranking score = estimated conversion rate + boosting scores from operational rules
    

- How will this model rank algorithmic, declarative and manual carousels?
    
- How will this model rank new carousel types (e.g. collections)?
    

### Eng Dependency:

- @xiaochang will validate if additional logging is required
    
- Better to assign unique ids to similar carousels
    
- Need to wait for Sibyl Migration to complete
    

  
  
  
  
  
**
